coredata.dc:title: Enhanced charge storage in supercapacitors using carbon nanotubes and N-doped graphene quantum dots-modified (NiMn)Co2O4
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Min
Lin, Huachen
Sun, Lin
Ying, Yulong
He, Bin
Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The integration of ternary metal oxides into carbon materials is anticipated to significantly boost the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor electrodes. This article synthesized carbon nanotubes (CNT)/(NiMn)Co2O4 composite materials using a straightforward hydrothermal method and subsequently prepared composite thin films of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD by phosphating and incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD). These films served as the functional electrode material for supercapacitors, enhancing their performance capabilities. The specific capacity of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD was measured at 2172.0 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, maintaining a capacitance of 1954.0 F g−1 at 10 A g−1, thus demonstrating excellent rate performance. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) further revealed enhancements in electrolyte flow dynamics and capacitance behavior post-NGQD introduction. The energy density of the composite material reached 94.4 Wh kg−1 at power density of 800 W kg−1, demonstrating superior electrochemical performance. The enhancement in these electrochemical properties is attributed to the high specific surface area and active sites of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD films, along with the synergistic effects of NGQD and metal ions facilitating rapid electrons and charge transfer. This work provides new insights into developing high-performance supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Carbon nanotubes
Transition metal oxides
Energy density
Film electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis diagram of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composite thin film.
(a-c) SEM images of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. (d-h) TEM and HRTEM images of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. (i-q) Element distribution mapping of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD.

(a) XRD patterns of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD, (b) XPS survey spectrum of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 and CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD, XPS spectra for (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) P 2p, (f) Ni 2p, (g) Co 2p, (h) Mn 2p and (i) N 1s of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 and CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composites film.

Comparison of (a-b) CV curves (2 mV s−1) and GCD curves of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. (c) CV curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD at different scan rates (2–100 mV s−1). (d) GCD curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD at different current density (1–10 A/g). (e) Capacity comparison diagram of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 and CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. (f) EIS curves of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 and CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD.
(a) CV curves at low scanning rate. (b) Linear fit line graph of log(i) and log(v). (c) Distribution of capacitive control in total capacitance. (d) Percentage of capacitive and surface control.

(a) Model diagram of assembled device, (b) CV curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD and AC in three-electrode system, (c) CV curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC at different voltage windows, (d) CV curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC at different scan rates (2–100 mV s−1); (e) GCD curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC at different current density (1–10 A g−1); (f) Specific capacitance of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC.

(a) Ragone plot of the asymmetric CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC device and other supercapacitors. (b) Practical application of the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC device, (c) Cycling performance and coulombic efficiency of the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD//AC device, (d) The morphological image after cycling.

head.ce:title: Enhanced charge storage in supercapacitors using carbon nanotubes and N-doped graphene quantum dots-modified (NiMn)Co2O4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control, Department of Materials Chemistry, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Environmental Functional Materials and Pollution Control, Department of Materials Chemistry, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A carbon nanotube and N-doped graphene quantum dot modified (NiMn) Co2O4 composite film was successfully prepared to improve the performance of supercapacitor electrode materials.

The integration of ternary metal oxides into carbon materials is anticipated to significantly boost the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor electrodes. This article synthesized carbon nanotubes (CNT)/(NiMn)Co2O4 composite materials using a straightforward hydrothermal method and subsequently prepared composite thin films of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD by phosphating and incorporating nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD). These films served as the functional electrode material for supercapacitors, enhancing their performance capabilities. The specific capacity of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD was measured at 2172.0 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, maintaining a capacitance of 1954.0 F g−1 at 10 A g−1, thus demonstrating excellent rate performance. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) further revealed enhancements in electrolyte flow dynamics and capacitance behavior post-NGQD introduction. The energy density of the composite material reached 94.4 Wh kg−1 at power density of 800 W kg−1, demonstrating superior electrochemical performance. The enhancement in these electrochemical properties is attributed to the high specific surface area and active sites of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD films, along with the synergistic effects of NGQD and metal ions facilitating rapid electrons and charge transfer. This work provides new insights into developing high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Addressing the challenge of developing safe and sustainable new energy is a major issue facing humanity. Energy storage devices are integral to daily lives, with dependence on them steadily increasing [1]. To cater to the escalating demand for energy storage and surmount the constraints posed by existing capacitors and batteries, researchers worldwide have engaged in extensive researches [2]. Supercapacitors, emerging as a highly promising renewable energy storage technology, offer advantages such as high- power capacity, efficient charge and discharge rates, long service life, and minimal environmental impact [3]. The energy storage in supercapacitors primarily depends on the charge distribution between the electrode and the electrolyte. According to their storage mechanisms, supercapacitors are divided into double-layer capacitors, pseudocapacitors, and hybrid capacitors [4]. There is ongoing effort to develop sustainable, inexpensive, and high-efficient materials for supercapacitor.

The performance of supercapacitors is fundamentally related to the capacity and working voltage of their electrodes. Therefore, materials selection and structural optimization are the key to surpass the energy storage constraints in supercapacitors [5]. Electrode materials must accommodate varying storage mechanisms, requiring attributes such as large specific surface area, low impedance, and robust chemical stability. Economic feasibility and environmental sustainability also play critical roles in material selection [6]. Typically, carbon materials, conductive polymers, and metal compounds are preferred due to their favorable properties. Carbon materials are notably prevalent, offering an extensive specific surface area, good conductivity, and chemical stability, making them popular choices for electrode materials [7]. Among them, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are distinguished by their tubular structure and sp2 orbital hybridization, endowing the material with high specific surface area, good mechanical elasticity, and superior conductivity. However, structural defects inherent in CNTs can decelerate electron migration rate, thus impairing their performance [8]. To solve this problem, heteroatoms such as N, P, S, and B are often incorporated into CNTs to markedly enhance their electrochemical properties [9,10]. In addition, compositing CNTs with other substances can augment the specific surface area, extend the potential window, and enhance conductivity [11].

Transition metal phosphides are recognized for their good conductivity, thermal conductivity, and excellent mechanical and electrochemical stability, making them promising electrode materials for supercapacitors [12,14,15]. However, the industrial application of single metal phosphide is limited by their low specific capacity [13]. To overcome this, phosphorus doping has been explored to enhance the surface wettability and electrochemical stability of carbon materials, achieving improved rate and cycling stability [16]. However, the low specific capacity of a single metal phosphide greatly restricts its industrial application [17]. Further advancements involve nitrogen doping, which effectively enhances the conductivity of carbon material frameworks, thereby boosting their electrical performance as supercapacitors electrodes [18]. This method not only increase the capacitance value, but also introduce additional pseudocapacitive effects, significantly improving the electrochemical performance and energy storage capacity of the devices [19]. Co-doping with N and P is particularly effective, inducing a synergistic effect that improves the performance of supercapacitors. This combined doping strategy optimizes the properties of both N-doped and P-doped carbon, leading to superior electrode materials [20,21].

In this work, we successfully prepared a CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD ternary composite system. The process began with the fine-tuning of reaction conditions to ensure the uniform morphology of the precursor. Subsequent surface modification with phosphides facilitated the introduction of phosphorus elements, enhancing the electrical properties of the materials. This modification is crucial for improving their rate capability and cycling stability. The synthesis continued with a chemical process designed to efficiently load NGQD at the nanoscale. This approach not only increased the specific surface area but also expanded the space available for N incorporation. The resulting CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD demonstrated a specific capacitance of 2172.0 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, demonstrating robust specific capacity. Additionally, it exhibits good cycling performance, maintaining 86.41 % of its specific capacitance with a coulombic efficiency of 97.92 % after 10,000 charge–discharge cycles. When paired with activated carbon (AC), the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composites exhibits a high energy density of 94.4 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, underscoring its potential as a superior electrode material for supercapacitors.

Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, glucose, glycerine, isopropanol, triammonium citrate, and sodium hydroxide all come from China National Pharmaceutical Group. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are got from Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. All chemicals utilized in this study were of analytical grade and were directly employed without further purification.

The precursors were obtained by a hydrothermal method. Initially, 0.0625 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.26 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.0625 mmol of Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, and 50 mg of glucose were combined in the beaker. Then, 8 ml of glycerin and 40 ml of isopropyl alcohol were added to the beaker. The solution was then subjected to ultrasonic treatment to ensure complete dispersion. Subsequently, 0.025 g of CNT was added to the above solution. The mixture was stirred thoroughly and then transferred to a reaction kettle. The reaction was conducted for 6 h at 180 °C. Upon completion, the kettle was allowed to cool to room temperature. The solution was then filtered to separate the carbon tube-containing film, which was subsequently placed in an oven to dry, yielding the final product.

A certain amount of sodium hypophosphite was prepared, quantified to be five times the mass of the precursor. Both the sodium hypophosphite and the precursor were placed separately into the porcelain boat. The boat containing the sodium hypophosphite was positioned in the upper section of the tubular furnace, while the boat with the precursor was positioned in the lower section. The furnace was set to a temperature of 350 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere and the materials were calcined for 2 h. Following the calcination, the furnace was allowed to cool to room temperature. After cooling, the sample was retrieved, marking the completion of the phosphidation process.

Dissolve 1 g of triammonium citrate in 20 ml of deionized water and heat it to 200 °C in the oil bath pot. After 30 min of reaction, the solution turns light orange, indicating the formation of NGQD precursors. Then add an appropriate amount of sodium hydroxide (10 mg mL−1) to neutralize the pH of the solution, resulting in NGQD. Soak the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 material in a 10-time diluted NGQD dispersion. After 15 min, the prepared CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD is repeatedly washed with ethanol and water and dried in the oven for 12 h to obtain the sample.

The synthesis of the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composite film is shown in Fig. 1 . Initially, the precursor CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4 is obtained by a hydrothermal method. During the process, CNTs are evenly interspersed within the nanospheres, forming a cohesive precursor structure. Subsequent phosphating of the precursor leads to the formation of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, where a nanosphere with the core shell structure was obtained. Following phosphating, the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 is immersed in dispersion of NGQD. Through static interaction, the NGQD uniformly adheres to the surface of the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 nanospheres. This adherence finalizes the formation of the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composite film, which serves as the positive electrode material in supercapacitors.

The microstructural features of the composite film were comprehensively characterized using scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. The SEM in Fig. 2 a shows that the Ni-Mn cobaltate within CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4 precursor is composed of nanospheres, with the CNT evenly interspersed throughout. Post-phosphating, the morphology of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 in Fig. 2b is retained, while the nanosphere structure of the precursor evolves into a core–shell structure (Fig. 2d) [22]. The incorporation of NGQD at room temperature led to successful loading onto the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, preserving its original morphology as shown in subsequent SEM analyses. TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) further elucidated the nanostructures. TEM images (Fig. 2d) confirmed the transformation of the nanosphere to a core–shell structure post-phosphating. The HRTEM images in Fig. 2e distinctly display the NGQD, predominantly spherical and varying in size, confirming successful loading onto the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composite film. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2f-h) reveals various lattice fringes, with the most obvious spacings measured at 0.207 nm and 0.202 nm, corresponding to the (400) crystal plane of MnCo2O4 and NiCo2O4, respectively. Element mapping (Fig. 2i-p) shows uniform distribution of Ni, Co, Mn, C, O, P, and N within the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD, indicating the successful synthesis of the composite film. The atomic ratio of Co: Ni: Mn is determined to be 7.01:3.43:3.19, closely approximating the stoichiometric ratio of 2:1:1, aligning with the designed composition of the NiCo2O4 and MnCo2O4 composites.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 3 a provides valuable insights into its crystalline structure. Notable peaks at 2θ = 44.622° and 2θ = 43.759° belong to the (400) crystal plane of NiCo2O4 and MnCo2O4, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis and high crystallinity of the material [23,24]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to further characterize the elements composition and chemical states within CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. The full XPS spectrum of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD (Fig. 3b) reveals the presence of 7 elements, including N, indicating the successful incorporation of NGQDs into the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 structure. This addition is evidenced by the changes in the surface chemical environment, detailed in subsequent XPS spectra. In the C 1s spectrum of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 and CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD (Fig. 3c), three distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.8 eV are identified, corresponding to CC, CO, and OCO bonding, respectively [25]. The O 1s spectrum of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composite film (Fig. 3d) shows three peaks at 531.5 eV, 532.1 eV and 533.3 eV, corresponding to MOM, MOH, and HOH bonds, highlighting alterations in the electron environment of oxygen atoms [26]. The P 2p spectrum in Fig. 3e demonstrates the attenuation peaks at 130.0 eV and 130.9 eV belonging to metal phosphide, with an additional peak at 134.0 eV due to partial surface oxidation when exposed to air [27]. In the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f), the binding energies of 855.4 eV (2p3/2) and 874.2 eV (2p1/2) are indicative of Ni2+, while the peaks at 857.3 eV (2p3/2) and 876.8 eV (2p1/2) suggest the presence of Ni3+ [28]. The slight forward shift in these peaks compared with CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4 suggests a strong interaction with NGQD, potentially enhancing electron transfer in the electrode. The Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3g) identifies peaks corresponding to Co2+ and Co3+ states, further validating the chemical changes due to NGQD interaction [29]. Finally, the Mn 2p spectrum in Fig. 3h is deconvolutionized into two components, Mn2+ and Mn3+, showcasing the detailed electronic structure [30]. In the N 1s region of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD (Fig. 3i), the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N at 399.1 eV, 400.1 eV, and 401.5 eV, respectively, confirms the nitrogen doping from NGQD, highlighting its impact on the composite's electronic properties [31].

The electrochemical behavior of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, and CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD was assessed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement within a potential window of 0–0.5 V. The integrated area of the CV curve, which is correlates with the specific capacitance of the electrode, was significantly larger for CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD compared to the other composites, indicating its superior capacitance (Fig. 4 a). Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves in Fig. 4b were obtained at a current density of 1 A g−1. Consistent with the CV results, the discharge time for CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD was notably longer, suggesting enhanced electrochemical performance due to the NGQD loading. The CV curves for CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD was recorded at a scan rate of 2–100 mV s−1 (Fig. 4c), maintains their shape across all rates, indicating excellent electrochemical kinetics of the materials. Additionally, the GCD curves of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD at varying current densities (Fig. 4d) exhibit clear pseudocapacitive charge–discharge plateaus, with nearly symmetrical charging and discharging profiles, indicating good reversibility of the redox processes. [32]. The specific capacitances calculated from charge-discharge tests are depicted in Fig. 4e. CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD exhibits the highest capacity of 2172.0 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, significantly higher than the other two electrodes. Even at a higher current density of 10 A g−1, the capacitance retention rates for CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD were 89.91 %, 87.18 %, and 89.96 %, respectively, indicating the robust rate performance of these Ni, Co, and Mn ternary metal oxide electrode materials.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were also used to understand the electron transfer and ion transport characteristics of the synthesized electrode (Fig. 4f). In the EIS spectrum, the semicircle in the high-frequency region represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct ) associated with the redox reactions at the electrode surface. Notably, CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD presented a smaller semicircle compared to the other two electrodes, indicating reduced charge transfer resistance. This finding demonstrates that loading of NGQD can improve the conductivity of the electrode. In the low-frequency region, the slope of the straight line is a significant impedance characteristic related to diffusion control, whereas the intercept of the X-axis reflects the contact internal resistance (Rs ). The CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD displayed the smallest contact resistance among the tested electrode, suggesting that the incorporation of NGQD promote better electrolyte flow dynamics within the electrode, thereby improving its capacitive behavior. Additionally, Zview software was intended to model an equivalent circuit to further analyze these impedance characteristics, as shown in the embedding diagram in Fig. 4f (the specific values of Rs and Rct are provided in Table S2 of the supporting information). During the energy storage process, the electrodes may undergo the following redox reactions [33]:
(1) (NiMn)Co2O4 + 4OH− ↔ 2CoOOH + NiOOH + MnOOH
(2) CoOOH + OH− ↔ CoO2 + H2O + e−
(3) NiOOH + OH− ↔ NiO2 + H2O + e−
(4) MnOOH + OH− ↔ MnO2 + H2O + e−

Further investigations into the electrochemical performance of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD were conducted using cyclic voltammetry (CV) at low scan rates. As shown in Fig. 5 a, each CV curve exhibited a pair of redox peaks. These peaks result from the interaction between OH− in the electrolyte solution and the working electrode, indicating Faraday reactions. In addition, slight shifts in the potential of the oxidation and reduction peaks, towards positive and negative directions respectively, were observed due to the electrode polarization and interface resistance, suggesting quasi-reversibility in the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD electrodes. The exponent 'b' in the power-law relationship between peak current and scan rate was calculated to assess the control mechanism of the electrochemical reactions. The calculation of b value is obtained from formula (1) in the supporting information. The 'b' value of 0.5 indicates a diffusion-controlled process, typical of battery-type materials, whereas a 'b' value of 1 suggests surface-controlled reactions, characteristic of pseudocapacitive material. Values between 0.5 and 1 indicate that both diffusion and capacitive processes are influencing the energy storage. For the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD, 'b' values of 0.7211 for the anodic peak and 0.7856 for the cathodic peak were observed (Fig. 5b), indicating that the charge storage mechanism is primarily capacitive but with significant diffusion control. The relative contributions of diffusion and capacitance mechanisms to the overall charge storage process were quantitatively assessed at various scan rates. For the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD, Fig. 5c depicts the increasing dominance of pseudocapacitance contribution at scan rates of 2, 3, 4, and 5 mV s−1, with value of 86.74 %, 88.10 %, 89.32 %, and 91.53 %, respectively (derived from formula (2) in the supporting information). This trend is further illustrated in Fig. 5d, where the relative contribution of capacitive control increases from 86.74 % to 91.53 % as the scan rate rises. This increase is attributed to the limited time available for electrolyte ions to diffuse throughout the electrode at higher scanning rates, which restricts their access to all active sites and consequently reduces the specific capacitance.

To gain insights into the real-world applications of the developed technology, a hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was meticulously constructed and subsequently assessed for its performance. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the assembly involved arranging electrode plates and a diaphragm in the sequence of “electrode plate-diaphragm-electrode plate” within the electrode shell. The interaction between electrodes and electrolytes is the key factor affecting storage efficiency and performance [34]. The assembly was immersed in 6 M KOH electrolyte to enable fully saturation of the diaphragm and electrode plate. CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD served as the positive electrode and AC as the negative electrode. The cell was then sealed to finalize the HSCs. The operational voltage window of the assembled HSCs was determinized via the CV testing in a three-electrode system (Fig. 6b). The CV curve exhibited polarization at potentials exceeding 1.6 V (Fig. 6c), establishing 0–1.6 V as the optimal voltage window for the device. Further CV testing in a two-electrode system revealed a rectangular shape in the low voltage range and oxidation–reduction peaks in the higher voltage range at high scanning rates (Fig. 6d). The lack of significant distortion at the highest scanning rate indicates excellent reversibility and satisfactory rate capability of the device.

The Galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves were systematically recorded at varying current densities, ranging from 1 A g−1 to 10 A g−1, within the designated potential window of 0–1.6 V, as depicted in Fig. 6e. All GCD curves exhibited triangular characteristics and good symmetry, indicating thigh coulombic efficiency. The specific capacitance of the device was measured as decreasing with increasing current density: 256.6, 239.9, 227.1, 200.6, 143.0, and 140.0 F g−1, respectively (Fig. 6f). These values underscore the robust rate performance of the HSC.

As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the hybrid supercapacitor achieves a maximum energy density of 94.4 Wh kg−1 and a maximum power density of 8000 W kg−1 (derived from formulas (3) and (4) in the supporting information). These metrics not only meet but in some cases exceed those reported in existing literature for similar HSC devices, as shown in Table 1 . This result clearly demonstrates the exceptional performance of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD. Demonstrating the practical utility of the HSC, Fig. 7b shows that two hybrid supercapacitors connected in series can power 14 green LED lights. Remarkably, the LED lights continue to emit light after 180 s, highlighting the potential of this device for real-world applications. In addition, the HSC device also exhibits robust cycling stability. After 10,000 cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1, the capacitance retention rate is 86.41 % and its coulombic efficiency is 97.92 % (Fig. 7c), the GCD curves of the first and last six cycles inserted in it exhibit good symmetry. This results indicate that the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD exhibits excellent durability and reversibility over extensive use.

SEM of the electrode after cyclic testing (Fig. 7d) reveals fractures on the surface of the film, likely due to external pressure during the assembly of the supercapacitor. Despite these fractures, the core structure of the nanospheres remains largely intact, indicating that the material’s structural integrity is preserved [35]. However, agglomeration of the nanospheres is observed, which could hinder ions diffusion into the material’s interior, slightly degrading its electrochemical performance.

This work introduces a novel approach involving the hydrothermal synthesis of CNT/(NiMn)Co2O4 precursors, followed by phosphating and incorporation of NGQD to produce CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composites. Through comprehensive characterization, the unique microstructure and elemental composition of these composites were elucidated, showcasing their enhanced electrochemical properties. The experimental results demonstarte that CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD has excellent electrochemical performance, characterized by a significant energy storage capacity. Specifically, the material achieved an energy density 94.4 Wh kg−1 at a the power density of 800 W kg−1. In terms of durability, the CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD device exhibits remarkable cycling stability and high Coulombic efficiency: it retained 86.4 % of its initial capacity even after 10,000 charge–discharge cycles, underscoring its excellent reversibility. This achievement not only provides an effective method for fabricating high-performance supercapacitor devices, but also lays a critical foundational basis for future research in this area. The synthesis strategy and the resultant supercapacitor performance highlight the potential of CNT/P-(NiMn)Co2O4@NGQD composites in advancing energy storage technologies.

Min Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Huachen Lin: Visualization, Investigation. Lin Sun: Validation, Software, Investigation. Yulong Ying: Formal analysis. Bin He: Validation, Software. Yu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22201107, 52203147), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220514) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2022M711380), and the Project Startup Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Jiangsu University (21JDG062).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.09.039.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
